title: Scarcity, opportunity cost, and the production possibilities frontier

questions.explanation: Opportunity cost is the value of the single next-best alternative you gave up — not the cash outlay alone (that misses implicit costs like forgone salary) and not the sum of every alternative (you could only have taken one of them).

Scarcity is the permanent condition that limited resources can't satisfy unlimited wants — it forces choice and exists even in rich economies. Empty shelves at a posted price are a shortage (a temporary market disequilibrium), and high prices or poverty are symptoms, not the definition.
Moving C→D gains 100 drones (200→300) and gives up 150 e-bikes (850→700). Opportunity cost per drone = 150 ÷ 100 = 1.5 e-bikes. The slope of the PPF segment is exactly this per-unit opportunity cost.

Only more resources or better technology move the frontier itself. Reallocating workers is a movement along the frontier; hiring the unemployed moves the economy from an interior point to the frontier (taking up slack, not growth); a preference change alters which point is chosen, not what is attainable.

Economic cost = explicit + implicit costs that change with the decision: tuition $60,000 + incremental housing ($18,000 − $12,000 = $6,000) + forgone salary $85,000 = $151,000. The $12,000 base rent is paid either way, and the $2,000 deposit is sunk — already gone under every alternative, so it doesn't count.

Comparative advantage follows the lower opportunity cost, not the higher output. A model costs Ben 6/3 = 2 forgone reports; it costs Ana 12/4 = 3. Ben gives up less, so models should go to Ben even though Ana out-produces him at everything (her edge is absolute advantage, which doesn't decide assignment).

The bow shape is the law of increasing opportunity cost: resources are specialized, so the first units of a good are made with resources well-suited to it, while later units must pull in resources that were far more productive elsewhere — each extra unit costs more of the other good. Utility and inflation are about consumption and prices, not the production trade-off.
